text: This video show laparoscopic totaliserectomy in case of scar abdomen.
This patient undergo a lap cholecystectomy and two previous C-section.
In this scar abdomen it's normal to find omental adhesion and it's better

The surgeon uses cold scissors to carefully free omental adhesions, controlling any bleeding before proceeding with the hysterectomy. The dissection involves finding the correct plane, with the adhesiolysis typically posing no risk to the bowel.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to immediately control a small bleeder, stopping the visible bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon clears the working area to ensure a safe and efficient total laparoscopic hysterectomy, removing any obstructions or adhesions that may impede the procedure.

The surgeon begins the hysterectomy by cutting the lateral attachments of the uterus, approximately 1.5 centimeters outside the uterine corner. In this postmenopausal woman, a bilateral adnexectomy is performed, requiring the opening of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon creates a small hole in the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament, carefully removing the surrounding peritoneum to improve hemostasis.

The surgeon cuts the peritoneum using the Clermont-Ferrand technique, creating a window just below the infundibulopelvic ligament to minimize ureteral trauma or injury. The bipolar device is used to assist in this maneuver.
The surgeon safely controls the infundibulopelvic ligament using a bipolar device, ensuring there is no risk to the nearby aorta.
The surgeon cuts the surrounding peritoneum tissue and uses a bipolar device to safely control both the artery and vein, ensuring no risk to adjacent structures.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the plane between the bladder and the vagina, carefully separating these structures to proceed with the hysterectomy.
The surgeon encounters a more complex dissection between the bladder and vagina due to the patient's history of two previous C-sections, which may have altered the anatomical planes.
The assistant pulls the uterus while the surgeon retracts the bladder cranially. The initial step involves removing the peritoneal tissue connecting the bladder and uterus.

The surgeon removes the bladder from its dense attachment to the uterus, following the dissection laterally on the right side. The peritoneum is opened from the round ligament to the infundibulopelvic ligament, mirroring the procedure performed on the opposite side.
The surgeon uses insufflation to aid in the dissection of connective tissue within the broad ligament, while applying cranial traction to the adnexa.

The surgeon cuts the peritoneum to increase the mobility of the adnexa, then applies cranial traction to create a window just below the infundibulopelvic ligament. This maneuver is crucial to reduce the risk of ureteral injury.

The surgeon cuts the peritoneum tissue surrounding the infundibulopelvic region, carefully controlling both the uterine artery and vein. The procedure progresses to a lateral dissection of the uterus.
The assistant uses scissors while the surgeon applies lateral traction on the left side of the uterus, facilitating dissection and separation of the surrounding tissues.

The surgeon attempts to increase the lateral mobility of the uterus and proceeds to cut the uterosacral ligament at its uterine insertion. In this case, the uterine artery is directly controlled before the vesico-uterine dissection.

The surgeon has fully controlled the uterine pedicle on the right side and proceeds with the mobilization of the vesico-uterine space, manipulating the surrounding tissues to separate the bladder from the uterus.
The surgeon carefully retracts the bladder cranially to prevent damage or perforation during the procedure.

The surgeon uses scissors to locate the correct dissection plane, pushing down the bladder on the uterine manipulator to cut the bladder pillars medially, ensuring avoidance of ureteral damage. The lateral dissection has been completed.
The surgeon exposes the right bladder pillar, clearly revealing the anatomical plane between the bladder and the vagina.
The surgeon prepares to perform a safe colpotomy by visualizing the anterior part of the uterine manipulator. The left bladder pilar is controlled, focusing on its medial part.

The surgeon controls the bladder pilar, allowing easy movement of the bladder. The bladder pilar is cut, revealing fibrotic tissue. The focus shifts to controlling the uterine pedicle on the left side.
The surgeon coagulates the uterine pedicle over a span of two to three centimeters using a bipolar cautery device, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon cuts the uterine pedicle, completing the separation of this structure from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon moves posteriorly to locate the uterine insertion of the left uterosacral ligament, preparing to dissect this anatomical structure.
The uterus is visibly devascularized, with its lateral and anterior attachments completely removed. The surgeon safely controls the left uterine pedicle, ensuring no risk or complications.
The surgeon controls the cervical vaginal vessel and proceeds with the colpotomy, cutting through the vaginal cuff to separate the cervix from the vagina.
The surgeon uses a monopolar needle to precisely section the edge of the vagina, enhancing the visibility and control during the procedure.

The surgeon cuts the anterior part of the vagina, starting with the left lateral section just medial to the uterine vessel, followed by the right lateral section. A monopolar needle is positioned on the left side.

The surgeon uses a trocar placed in the left lateral position to perform the posterior part of the colpotomy. The incision is made on the lateral part of the vagina, and the monocular needle is visible in the left lateral trocar as the colpotomy is completed in the posterior vaginal area.
The surgeon completes the colpotomy, fully incising the vaginal cuff. The small uterus is ready for vaginal extraction without requiring reduction maneuvers.
The surgeon prepares to extract the uterus through the vaginal canal following the completion of the colpotomy.
The surgeon begins closing the edge of the vagina using a 2-0 Vicryl stitch, starting at the lateral part of the vaginal incision.
The surgeon prepares to close the vaginal incision using an extracorporeal knotting technique, employing the rudder knot method.
A small bleeder is visible on the edge of the vaginal incision, which is left untreated to promote better healing.
The surgeon begins closing the vaginal cuff using a 2-0 Vicryl suture, starting at the lateral part of the vagina and proceeding to the medial part for optimal cicatrization.

The surgeon ensures the entire mucosa is included within the suture to minimize the risk of postoperative vaginal bleeding. The number of stitches varies based on the size of the vagina, typically using four or five stitches.
The surgeon carefully places the vaginal mucosa inside the stitch during the closure process to minimize the risk of post-operative bleeding.
The surgeon performs an intracorporeal knotting technique to achieve a perfect closure of the vaginal cuff, ensuring the edges are neatly approximated.
This is our technique for a scar abdomen.
